Accessibility
Accessibility
Foundation guide
Ace Grid already handles grid roles, focus, and live ARIA state. On this page, you only configure the name and description props.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `accessibility` block into your own gridProps object.
Foundation
Accessibility
Root accessibility metadata
Ace Grid already handles the grid role plus live row, column, focus, selection, and busy state. The public accessibility config is only three props: `ariaLabel`, `ariaLabelledBy`, and `ariaDescribedBy`.
Use `ariaLabelledBy` when the page already has a visible heading that should name the grid.
Use `ariaLabel` when there is no visible heading to reference.
Use `ariaDescribedBy` only when users need extra context before they start navigating cells.
Switch the grid name between a visible heading, a direct aria-label, and the default fallback.
Turn the description on only when it adds useful task context.
Use the page summary to compare built-in support with the three props you actually configure.
Prefer real visible headings and helper text over hidden accessibility-only copy.
If both aria-label and aria-labelledby are present, the linked visible label should win.
Keep the name short. Keep the description focused on what the grid is for.

Charts
Overview
Visualization guide
Select a range, then right-click Create chart. This page shows manual chart creation, live updates, and brush-to-filter.
Use this page before chart panel, analysis, or appearance settings.
Visualization
Charts
Integrated charts
Charts turn a selected range into a visual. The chart opens from the context menu and can update when data or selection changes.
Use this when the current selection is the source of truth for the chart, but you want chart creation to stay manual.
Keep it as the single entry point when you do not need a separate selection page.
Keep auto-update on when chart values should track edits.
Use Brush key, drag across the chart, then click Filter rows.
Right-click the selection and choose Create chart to open the panel.
Use `charts.autoUpdate` when the chart should react to data edits as well as selection changes.
Use `selection.enableCellSelection` and `selection.selectEntireColumnOnSelection` to shape the active range.
Use `charts.autoUpdateSelection`, `charts.createChartMenu`, and `charts.controls.enableBrushSelection` to keep chart opening under the user's control.
Use `charts.initialBrushRowIds`, `charts.controls.brushActions`, `charts.controls.brushSelectionModifier`, and `charts.onBrushSelection` for brush-driven filtering.

entries.content:
Column Grouping
Column Grouping
Structure guide
Column grouping lets you organize related columns under shared headers. You can also track which groups stay open and keep sibling columns moving together during drag.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `columnGrouping` block into your own gridProps object.
Structure
Column Grouping
Column grouping configuration
Column grouping adds a shared band above related columns so users can read wider datasets without losing the structure of the data.
Use it when the grid has clear column families such as quarterly numbers, actual versus forecast, or current versus previous state.
It is most helpful once the table grows beyond a few standalone columns and users need help understanding the groups at a glance.
Collapse and expand the header bands to confirm what the host app can persist.
Switch between the shallow and three-level examples to decide how much grouping users actually need before the header gets too tall.
Compare default-open and default-collapsed groups to decide whether users are usually detail-first or summary-first.
Use the public callback when the page needs to remember which header bands a user opened.
Do not group columns just because the schema can be grouped. Only add bands that improve scanning.

entries.content:
System columns
System columns
Structure guide
System columns place built-in helper columns without cloning the base data columns.
Use this page for system column placement. Grouping, pinning behavior, resizing, and selection rules each have their own feature pages.
Structure
Columns
Column-level configuration (width map)
Columns control the screen-level presentation of the base column definitions, especially width maps and the order, pinning, and size of built-in helper columns.
Use this area when the same business columns appear in several screens but need different emphasis on each screen.
Keep these props close to the view when a revenue screen, audit screen, and support screen need different width priorities or helper-column placement.
Widen the most important numeric columns and compare how quickly users can scan totals or margins.
Move helper columns between the pinned strip and the center-leading band to confirm the screen still reads clearly either way.
Turn selection, row pinning, row reorder, and detail toggles on one at a time to verify each helper column enters the layout and keeps its matching interaction live.
Put reusable business meaning in the base column defs and screen-specific emphasis in `columns`.
Prefer a small set of intentional helper-column rules over one-off tweaks. Too much local sizing becomes hard to maintain.

Edit
Interaction guide
Editing turns the grid from a reader into an editor. This page focuses on enabling edits cleanly and reacting to user changes without breaking grid state.

Interaction
Edit
Editing feature (cells and row ops)
Editing turns the grid into the place where users change data, not just view it. The main job here is making edits feel immediate and safe.
Use inline editing on worklists, planning views, and operational screens where the grid is the main editing surface.
Prefer read-only grids on reporting screens where direct edits would be surprising or too easy to trigger by mistake.
Edit a few obvious fields, such as status or revenue, and confirm that the host state updates as soon as the user commits the change.
Try editing several cells quickly so you can feel whether the update loop stays responsive under repeated changes.
Keep edit handlers close to the owning state so the grid reflects committed changes right away.
If edits can fail, decide early whether the grid should block, revert, or save optimistically and then validate.

Formula bar feature
Formula settings shape the spreadsheet layer, especially whether users get a visible formula bar and how references behave when rows or columns move.
Use it on spreadsheet-like planning and modeling screens where formulas are part of the user workflow.
Skip it on simple read-only reports where formulas exist behind the scenes but users never need to inspect or edit them directly.
Switch between keyed and positional reference modes and think about how often rows or columns move in the real workflow.
Toggle the formula bar and decide whether it helps users inspect calculations or only adds surface area.
Keyed references are usually safer when the table shape changes because they stay tied to field meaning instead of physical position.
Keep formula UX visible only where users truly act like spreadsheet users; otherwise the extra controls feel heavy.

entries.content:
Keyed Headers
Keyed Headers
Structure guide
Keyed headers add a spreadsheet-style coordinate layer to the grid: letters across columns and numbers down rows. Use them when people need to point to cells as B4 or D12 instead of only naming the business column. Turn on annotation when you want to keep the coordinate and the original header visible together.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `keyedHeaders` block into your own gridProps object.
Structure
Keyed Headers
Spreadsheet-style row/column keys
Keyed headers add a lightweight coordinate layer on top of the normal labels, so users can point to cells as A1, B4, or C12 without losing the real business headers.
Use them for spreadsheet migration, support scripts, QA steps, and training material where people naturally say things like A3 or B12.
They also help on dense operational screens when a short coordinate is easier to share in chat, tickets, or walkthroughs than a long field name.
Skip them on dashboards and simple reports where business labels already do the job and extra coordinates would add noise.
Turn on column keys first if users mainly need fast column references. Add row keys only when row position matters too.
Use annotation when people are still learning the sheet or when the short key should stay attached to the business label, for example A : Date.
Compare the page with keyed headers on and off. Keep them only when users actually speak in coordinates during the task.
Test them alongside selection, copy and paste, or editing flows, because that is where cell references usually become valuable.
Keyed headers work best with selection, clipboard, editing, validation, and formula-driven screens where users already think cell by cell.
Column keys are usually the first thing spreadsheet users expect. Row keys matter more when visible row order is part of the workflow too.
Keep the normal business headers strong. The coordinate layer should help orientation, not replace meaningful labels.

Row Grouping
Data Models guide
Row grouping turns flat rows into collapsible sections. This page keeps the demo focused on the client-side action layer: nested groups, row selection plus drag reordering, and the callbacks that keep those interactions in host-owned state.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `rowGrouping` block into your own gridProps object.
Data Models
Row Grouping
Row grouping behaviour
Row grouping is driven by the action layer. The grouped rows usually come from `useGrid`, and the public `rowGrouping` prop wires the grid's group toggles and drag actions back into that same host-owned state.
Use grouping on large datasets that users naturally read by team, region, owner, or another shared business dimension.
It is best when grouped rows help users scan or act faster, not just because the data can be grouped.
Use the page controls to switch between single-level and nested grouping layouts, change the label column, and turn row selection or row reorder on and off.
Click group rows to expand or collapse them, drag selected rows into another group, and drag a top-level group row to reorder groups. The action cards only cover expand all, collapse all, and reset.
Create and manipulate groups through `useGrid` or your own host action layer.
Pass `api.toggleGroupExpansion`, `api.expandAllGroups`, `api.collapseAllGroups`, `api.moveRowsToGroup`, and `api.reorderRowGroups` into `rowGrouping` so built-in group UI and row dragging can call them.

Infinite Scroll
Infinite Scroll
Data Models guide
Infinite scroll controls cover incremental loading. Use them when the full data set is too large to keep in memory all at once.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `scroll` block into your own gridProps object.
Data Models
Scroll
Scrolling / infinite loading
Scroll controls cover incremental loading for stream-like experiences where users keep moving forward through a long list.
Use it when a load-more or infinite model matches how the screen is consumed.
Prefer pagination when users think in discrete pages and expect a stable total range instead of a stream.
Load several batches in sequence and check whether the page still feels predictable once the visible window grows.
Try a smaller and larger batch size to see the trade-off between frequent loading and heavier payloads.
Design the loading state as part of the UX, not as an afterthought.
The grid only requests the next slice. Your host app still owns the row window and must push the updated rows back into `data.rows`.

Search
Search (highlight/filter)
Search helps users find text inside the current grid. It can either highlight matches in place or reduce the grid to matching rows only.
Use highlight mode when users need context and still want to compare nearby rows.
Use filter mode when the page should turn into a short list of matches.
Try the same query in highlight mode and filter mode so the difference is easy to see.
Turn on case-sensitive or whole-word matching when short terms would otherwise match too broadly.
Test a term users will actually search for, such as a region, owner, or status.
Search should complement filters, not replace them. Use search for quick find tasks and filters for deliberate narrowing.

SSRM selection keeps select-all state even when only some rows are loaded.

SSRM selection stores a stable selection state object instead of treating the loaded rows as the full truth.
Use it when select-all needs to include rows that are not loaded in memory yet.
Choose controlled SSRM selection when the host needs to persist, inspect, or replay selection state.
Click row checkboxes and watch the SSRM selection state update below the grid.

SSRM Tree Data
Data Models guide
Use SSRM tree data when each record already belongs to a server-owned path.
See route expansion and path-shaped child requests.
Data Models
Server Row Model
Server-side row model (block cache)
SSRM tree data keeps the hierarchy on the server. The grid asks for direct children of a route instead of flattening the whole tree in the browser.
Use it for folders, org charts, nested work breakdowns, and any data model where parent-child structure is part of the record itself.
Choose tree data over row grouping when the hierarchy already exists in the source data instead of being built interactively by the user.
Expand a root node, then a child node, and watch the route request move deeper into the tree.
Use the refresh buttons to re-request a specific route without reloading the whole root store.
Tree-data SSRM routes address direct children for the current path, not the whole visible tree.
Keep the server response aligned to the requested route so the grid can stitch child stores into the visible outline.

Sorting
Sorting
Data Operations guide
Sorting changes row order. This page shows how one sort rule and stacked sort rules change the same table in client and server mode.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `sorting` block into your own gridProps object.
Data Operations
Sorting
Sorting
Sorting changes row order. It is one of the fastest ways to turn the same table into a ranking, triage list, or review queue.
Use `sortColumn` + `sortDirection` when the page only needs one active ranking at a time.
Use one sort rule when users mainly care about one ranking, such as highest revenue or lowest margin.
Use more than one rule when the page needs a stable review order, such as status and then revenue.
Start with a useful seed sort, then click headers to see how the active sorting state changes.
Hold Shift, then click a second header when you want to keep the first rule and add another one.
Check whether the page needs a fixed default order or whether users should build their own order on the fly.
Keep the default order aligned with the main question users bring to the page.
If the final data comes from the server, test the same sort UX in server mode before shipping.

Spanning
Spanning
Structure guide
Spanning merges related cells into larger visual blocks. Use it sparingly when grouped labels, status banners, or handoff strips are easier to scan than repeated row-by-row cells.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `spanning` block into your own gridProps object.
Structure
Spanning
Cell spanning / merged cells
Spanning merges multiple cells into a larger visual block. It works best when the merged region communicates one idea, such as a grouped label, a status banner, or an ownership handoff.
Use it for banners, merged labels, grouped summaries, or compact status strips that would be noisy if repeated in every row.
Pair it with obvious cell-selection behavior so merge and unmerge feel local to the selected range instead of like hidden global commands.
Toggle spanning on and off and ask whether the merged block makes the table easier or harder to scan.
Switch between the Program labels, Status banners, and Ops handoffs examples, then right-click a selected range to see whether Merge cells or Unmerge cells is available.
Only merge when the visual gain is real. Repeated values alone are not a good enough reason.
Define how merged regions behave with row identity, sorting, and selection before using them widely. Some system cells, including pin and reorder helpers, may disable themselves around spans.

Sparkline
Sparkline
Visualization guide
Sparklines render compact trend visuals directly inside cells. This page keeps the dataset small and gives the chart-backed types more room so you can compare the full catalog without extra chrome.
Compare line, area, bar, column, win/loss, stacked column, stacked bar, histogram, heatmap, box, violin, waterfall, bullet, candlestick, pie, donut, scatter, and bubble sparklines side by side in the live demo, then copy the matching `sparkline` block into your own `gridProps` object.
Visualization
Sparkline
Sparkline rendering
Sparklines keep a compact signal inside the row so users can read direction, shape, or distribution without leaving the table.
Use the showcase to compare the full catalog before you pick the shape that fits the story.
Keep the dataset small and the row labels clear so the chart type, not the data volume, stays front and center.

Toggle the viewer only if the inline chart needs a larger surface for detail or interaction.
Treat sparklines as signal, not decoration. If the trend is not important enough to influence action, skip it.

Theming
Foundation guide
Theming lets you match Ace Grid to your product UI without rewriting the grid internals. Use it for tokens, icons, and targeted style overrides.

Foundation
Theming
Theme (tokens & icons)
Theming changes how the grid looks without changing what the data means. Use it for product tokens, icons, and a few scoped visual overrides.
Use it when the grid should feel native to your design system instead of like a third-party embed.
Reach for theme tokens first, then use targeted style overrides only for the few selectors tokens cannot cover.
Compare the base liquid-glass theme against a version with accented headers or other light brand touches.
Try the same feature config in both light and dark theme so you can confirm the data remains just as readable.
Keep visual identity in the theme layer. Avoid pushing product styling into data, column, or interaction props.
Use inline selector overrides sparingly. They are best for surgical polish, not a second theming system.

Tree Data
Data Models guide
Tree data is for record-owned hierarchies such as folders, org charts, or nested projects. This page shows where the path comes from, how the tree renders it, and how that differs from Row Grouping.
Use the live example to compare settings, then lift the matching `treeData` block into your own gridProps object.
Data Models
Tree Data
Client-side tree data (AG Grid parity style).
Tree data is for records that already belong to a hierarchy, such as folders, org charts, or nested projects.
Use it when each row already has a stable parent-child route in the business model.
Prefer row grouping when users should be able to create groups from otherwise flat rows during the session.
Compare an array path field, a delimited path field, and a resolver so the hierarchy contract stays obvious.
Toggle default expansion and check whether users should start in a summary view or a detail-first view.
Tree Data reads a route from the records; Row Grouping creates UI groups from flat rows.

Data validation
Validation decides when edit rules run and what feedback users see when a value does not meet the expected rule.

Foundation guide
Virtualization controls how much of the grid stays mounted at once. In most cases, leave it on.

Foundation
Virtual
Virtualization strategy flags
Virtualization controls how much of the grid stays mounted at once. It reduces browser work on long or wide grids.
Keep all three virtual settings on for most production grids.
Only turn one off when a custom cell or embedded widget truly needs a wider mounted window.
Turn row virtualization off and compare the mounted row count against the default mode.
Turn horizontal virtualization off and compare the mounted column count on the same wide grid.
Turn cell-content virtualization off and compare how many heavy cells stay mounted.
Treat virtualization as a performance setting, not a visual feature.
Validate custom cell renderers early. They are the most common reason teams try to change virtualization defaults.

examples.label: React
examples.summary: React mounts straight from the runtime tier, so you only choose the tier package and optional schema tooling.

examples.actionsSummary: Use `ViewChild` to get the wrapper instance, then call `getActions()` from your component.
examples.label: Angular
examples.summary: Angular uses the dedicated wrapper package while keeping the same runtime tiers and schema add-ons behind it.

examples.actionsSample:
<script setup lang="ts">
import { ref } from "vue";
import { AceGrid } from "@ace-grid/vue";

const grid = ref<any>(null);
const gridProps = {
  data: {
    rows,
    columns,
  },
  layout: {
    width: 960,
    height: 420,
  },
};

function addRow() {
  grid.value?.getActions()?.appendRow({
    data: {
      make: { value: "Tesla" },
      status: { value: "Draft" },
    },
  });
}
</script>

<template>
  <button @click="addRow">Add row</button>
  <AceGrid ref="grid" :props="gridProps" />
</template>
examples.actionsSummary: Use a component ref and call `getActions()` from your Vue event handlers.
examples.label: Vue
examples.summary: Vue ships through the wrapper package, but the runtime contract and enterprise tier model stay the same.

examples.actionsSample:
<script lang="ts">
  import AceGrid from "@ace-grid/svelte";

  let grid: any;
  const gridProps = {
    data: {
      rows,
      columns,
    },
    layout: {
      width: 960,
      height: 420,
    },
  };

  function addRow() {
    grid?.getActions()?.appendRow({
      data: {
        make: { value: "Tesla" },
        status: { value: "Draft" },
      },
    });
  }
</script>

<button on:click={addRow}>Add row</button>
<AceGrid bind:this={grid} props={gridProps} />
